                           Multilateral organisations?




4




                                                                                                                                                          2
                                                                                                                                                10/01/2026




                                             Hybrid organisations

     Hybridity is a term used within the social impact field to refer to the merging of commercial revenue generation (traditionally thought
     as part of businesses) and a central social cause (traditionally the domain of non-profits).

     Hybrid organisations sit in a spectrum between nonprofit and business, mixing social impact and commercial
     revenue, in an effort to develop financially sustainable enterprises that ‘primarily pursue a social mission but rely
     significantly on commercial revenue to sustain operations’ (Battilana et al. 2012, p. 51).




5




     Who creates social impact – hybrid organisations
            Government                      Non-profits                     Business                      Individuals



                                                     Hybrid - Social Enterprise
    Social Enterprise & Hybridity
       In the sphere of social impact, hybridity refers to the combination of
       commercial revenue generating (traditionally associated with enterprises) and
       a major social cause (traditionally the domain of nonprofits).
       These hybrid organisations are sometimes called social enterprises, however
       as this sector is new, industry and academic literature have contradictory
       definitions. Industry uses terminology like "social business" and "purpose-
       driven businesses" to refer to hybrid organisations at different places on the
       spectrum.




6




                                                                                                                                                       3
                                                                                                                                               10/01/2026




                                    New sector, many names!

       Hybrid organisations are often referred to as social enterprises, but since this field is emerging, there are competing
       definitions of the term 'social enterprise' in industry and academic literature.

       Definitions also differ from one geography to the next.

       Terms like 'social business' and 'purpose-driven businesses' have also been taken up in industry to refer to some of these
       hybrid organisations, each referring to organisations that sit in different places in the hybrid spectrum.




                          Purpose-                  Purpose-
     Social                                                                   Impact                                               Social
                           Driven                     First                                             B Corp
    Business                                                                 Business                                            Enterprise
                          Business                  Business




7




                                New sector, many questions!
    • What's the legal structure of a social enterprise?
    • How are they financed?
    • Is a social enterprise different to CSR?
    • Do they have to distribute their profits?
    • Do they have both customers and beneficiaries?
    • How do they define and measure social value?
    • What are their cultures like?




8




                                                                                                                                                      4
                                                                                        10/01/2026




                           One definition of social enterprise
     •Social Traders – Australia's leading social enterprise certification
     scheme – identifies three key components:
     • Having a defined primary social, cultural or environmental purpose
       consistent with a public or community benefit.
     • Deriving a substantial portion of their income from trade.
     • Investing efforts and resources into their purpose such that
       public/community benefit outweighs private benefit.*

     • *Please note, 50% of profits donated to social cause is also widely accepted.




9




                                 Social enterprise in Australia
     •Australia has over 12,000 social enterprises.
     •Social enterprises contribute $21.3 billion to the Australian economy
     and account for 1% of GDP.
     •Over 206,000 people are employed in Australian social enterprises.
     That's one in 60 jobs, or 1.6% of the workforce (Social Enterprise
     Australia 2022).




10




                                                                                               5
                                                                                                                                        10/01/2026




       Market Failure & Unmet Social Needs

       •Market failure refers to a movement away from the economically
       efficient outcome (that is where mutually beneficial trades could be
       made)
       Market Failure Guide, NSW Government, Dec 2017

       An unmet social need is a term used that helps to describe social
        disadvantage, inequality and poverty which outcomes can be linked
        with market failures

       • The nature of the marketplaces that exist in every society is different.
       Universally, negative social outcomes and unmet social demands such
         as poverty and homelessness result from market failures,
         inefficiencies, and excesses.



11




       Examples of Market Failure
     • 1. High electricity prices
     • In 2018, the Australian Energy Market Commission (AEMC) found that electricity prices in Australia had increased by 56%
       over the previous five years, well above the rate of inflation. This was due to a number of factors, including the closure of
       coal-fired power plants, investment in renewable energy, and network congestion.
     • The high electricity prices placed a significant burden on households and businesses, particularly those on low incomes.
       In some cases, it led to people being unable to afford to power their homes or businesses.
     • The government responded to the market failure by introducing a number of measures to reduce electricity prices,
       including:
     • Investing in renewable energy and battery storage
     • Upgrading the electricity grid
     • Providing subsidies to low-income households




12




                                                                                                                                               6
                                                                                                                                         10/01/2026




       Examples of Market Failure
     • 2. Lack of affordable housing
     • The cost of housing in Australia has increased significantly in recent years, making it difficult for many people to afford to
       buy or rent a home. This is due to a number of factors, including limited supply, low interest rates, and increasing
       demand.
     • The lack of affordable housing has led to a number of social problems, including homelessness, overcrowding, and
       poverty. It has also made it difficult for people to move to areas where there are more job opportunities.
     • The government has responded to the market failure by introducing a number of measures to increase the supply of
       affordable housing, including:
     • Providing subsidies to developers to build affordable housing
     • Investing in social housing
     • Releasing government land for affordable housing development




13




       Examples of Market Failure
     • 3. Poor access to mental health services
     • Mental health is a major issue in Australia, with one in five people experiencing a mental illness each year. However, there
       is a shortage of mental health professionals and services, particularly in rural and regional areas.
     • The lack of access to mental health services has led to a number of problems, including people not being able to get the
       help they need, waiting long periods of time for appointments, and having to travel long distances to see a mental health
       professional.
     • The government has responded to the market failure by increasing funding for mental health services, including:
     • Expanding the Medicare Benefits Schedule to cover more mental health services
     • Investing in early intervention and prevention programs
     • Increasing the number of mental health professionals in rural and regional areas
     • These are just three examples of market failures that have occurred in Australia over the last 5 years. The government has
       intervened in all of these cases to address the unmet social needs and improve the outcomes for Australians.




14




                                                                                                                                                7
                                                                                 10/01/2026




     Turning unmet social needs to market
     opportunities
      • Unmet social need assists in framing social disadvantage, inequality,
        and poverty in a manner that connects these negative social
        outcomes to market failures. They can be viewed as commercial
        opportunities if they are reframed as unsatisfied market
        requirements (rather than as solely human or government problems).
      • This perspective helps us recognise how capitalist markets, from
        production to distribution, may contribute and play a vital role in
        solving unmet social need in new and interesting ways.
      • One of the core assumptions of social entrepreneurs is that it is
        possible, via innovation, to create markets that "operate for social
        benefit."



15




                    CHATGPT: What is a problem?

     Understanding the problems, we face
     • Positive social impact begins with determining the nature and type of the problem you are attempting to
       tackle, as different sorts of problems demand different types of answers. Therefore, "understanding the
       context, setting, and stakeholders in which an intervention, program, organisation, company, or agency
       operates is essential for improved assessment, evidence, and impact" (CSI 2014, p.14).


                                            The three type of problems
             Simple problems                Complicated problems           Complex (wicked)
                                                                           problems
             • Predictable & linear in      • Predictable & linear in      • Are nonlinear in nature
               nature                         nature                         and unpredictable
             • Use best practice to         • But have multiply            • Can have a variety of
               solve                          components                     causes and solutions
                                            • Different ways to solve      • Can also produce
                                                                             unintended
                                                                             consequences



17




                                   Cynefin Framework
         •Simple, Complicated, Chaotic and Complex.




18




                                                                                                                         9
                                                                                                                                               10/01/2026




                                             Wicked Problems
      • Wicked problems have no definitive formulation (e.g. two types of poverty are not the same)
     • Wicked problems involve many stakeholders with different values and priorities (e.g. drought)
     • Wicked problems have no ‘stopping rule’: the search for solutions never stops and the problem
                                       never ends (e.g. addiction)
                          • Wicked problems' roots are complex and tangled (e.g. terrorism)
            • Wicked problems are unprecedented and constantly changing (e.g. climate change)

      •   References: Camillus, J. (2008) ‘Strategy as a wicked problem,’ Harvard Business Review, May 2008; Rittel, H. W., & Webber, M. M.
                               (1973) ‘Dilemmas in a general theory of planning,’ Policy Sciences, 4(2), pp. 155-169.




19




                                  Example: Homelessness

      'It is critical to consider the system
        you intend to change, and within
     which the person or group exists and
     that will be impacted by the change’
               Reference: The Compass CSI (2014, p. 15)




22




                                                                 11
                                                                                                          10/01/2026




               Important characteristics of systems:
     1.   Independent parts play different roles in the system (i.e some parts are inputs, some parts
          are outputs, some parts are catalysts, some parts are all three).
     2.   Parts are interconnected (i.e parts impact and interact with one another).
     3.   Interactions between parts can create circular feedback loops (i.e dimension 1 of the
          problem affects dimension 2 which in turn reinforces or dampens dimension 1).
     4.   Systems like balance so will form feedback loops to support this balance (i.e an established
          system's feedback loops strengthen and "fight for" the status quo).
     5.   The total is greater than the sum of its parts (i.e the way different parts combine adds a
          different quality to the singular parts).

     Wright, Diana, and Donella H. Meadows. Thinking in Systems : A Primer, Taylor & Francis Group,
     2009.




23




          How does all of this relate to our clients?
               •   Social entrepreneurs ‘look for – and often find – solutions to
                insoluble problems in the unlikeliest places. They are driven by
              passion to expand business thinking to reach people in need. Thus,
               many are pioneering and helping map out future markets where
                  most of us would only see nightmarish problems and risk’

                                   • Reference: Elkington and Hartigan (2008, p. 6)

      What Surf Lifesaving Sydney seek
     Last week Gary made clear that the issue they want us to address is
     additional funding. You need to focus on Surf Lifesaving Sydney.

     They have lost a $200,000 donor and this impacts the services they
     provide.

     Focus on ways to address this with viable, concrete suggestions as to
     how they can raise the money needed.




29




                         Proceed with caution...




30




                                                                                    15
                                                                                                                                        10/01/2026




     What is a project scope?
      The project scope is the total amount of work that needs to be done to
      complete a project.

      Defining scope is an essential part of the project planning process as it
      helps teams or project managers to determine what the project goals,
      deliverables, tasks, costs and deadlines are.

      Most importantly, it ensures you and your client are on the same page.



      Reference: Project Manager (2018)




31




                                                  Work Breakdown Structure (WBS)

     1. Define the Objectives
     Your project objectives set the rules for defining your project scope. They will be informed by the project brief and your
     meetings with the clients.

     2. Identify Project Phases
     The next level down is the project phases: break your objectives into a series of phases that will take it from conception to
     completion.

     3. List Your Project Deliverables
     What are your project deliverables? What is the client expecting from you? List them all and note the work needed for those
     project deliverables to be deemed successfully delivered (sub-deliverables, resources, participants, etc.). Try and put them in
     order from first to last.

     4. Create Work Packages
     Take your deliverables from above and break them down into every single task and subtask that is necessary to deliver
     them. Group those into work packages.

     5. Understanding What’s Possible
     With the tasks now laid out, you can begin to understand what is going to be possible in the time you have remaining in the
     term. This is where you start to play with and prioritise your activities and timelines.
